findings.claim: Temporal split (months 1-12, 13-15, 16-18) prevents campaign leakage AND campaign-level stratification ensures no campaign is split across folds
findings.failure_mechanism: Spear-phishing campaigns often span multiple months. If campaigns cross temporal boundaries (e.g., months 12-13 or 15-16), you cannot simultaneously do temporal split AND ensure no campaign is split. Either the temporal split is violated (leakage) or the campaign stratification claim is false. This directly invalidates the leakage prevention mechanism.
findings.evidence_test: Extract campaign identifiers from the dataset and check if any campaign spans temporal boundaries (months 12-13 or 15-16). If campaigns cross boundaries, the claim is false.

findings.claim: Labels with 7-day dwell time requirement are complete for all test set emails
findings.failure_mechanism: Test set includes months 16-18. If evaluation happens before all test emails have 7 days of data (e.g., evaluation during month 18), labels are incomplete. Incomplete labels could leak information or cause eval_inflation if partial labels are used as ground truth.
findings.evidence_test: Check label timestamps for all test set emails. If any test email has label timestamp within 7 days of email timestamp, labels are incomplete for that email.

findings.claim: Transformer outperforms baseline for detecting spear-phishing
findings.failure_mechanism: Baseline uses sender reputation and header features, transformer uses text + attachment metadata. These are fundamentally different feature sets. Improvement could be due to text features, not transformer architecture. Without a transformer baseline with same features as baseline, the claim is ambiguous.
findings.evidence_test: Run transformer with same features as baseline (no text). If performance is similar to baseline, text is the driver, not architecture.

findings.claim: Hyperparameter tuning on validation set (3 months) produces stable model selection
findings.failure_mechanism: 3 months of data might be too small for stable hyperparameter selection, especially with cross-validation. Could lead to overfitting to validation set and poor generalization to test set.
findings.evidence_test: Compare hyperparameter stability across different validation set splits. If results vary significantly, validation set is too small.

findings.claim: Email body text truncated to 512 subword tokens is sufficient for phishing detection
findings.failure_mechanism: Spear-phishing emails might have important indicators beyond 512 tokens (e.g., detailed social engineering narratives, long URLs, embedded content). Truncation could miss critical signals and bias results.
findings.evidence_test: Analyze misclassified emails for length. If many failures are from long emails, truncation is a problem.

findings.claim: Campaign-level stratification ensures no campaign is split across folds
findings.failure_mechanism: Document doesn't explain how campaigns are identified. Without clear methodology, this claim cannot be verified. Campaign identification could be arbitrary or inconsistent.
findings.evidence_test: Request campaign identification methodology. If not provided or unclear, claim is unverifiable.

findings.claim: Threat intelligence reputation scores frozen at end of training period are sufficient for production
findings.failure_mechanism: Production deployment will have updated threat intelligence. Model trained on frozen scores might not generalize to production where threat intelligence is dynamic.
findings.evidence_test: Compare model performance with frozen vs. live threat intelligence scores on test set.

findings.claim: Attachment metadata (file extension, MIME type, size) is sufficient for phishing detection
findings.failure_mechanism: More sophisticated attachment analysis (content, macros, etc.) might be important for phishing detection. Limited metadata could miss signals.
findings.evidence_test: Compare performance with and without attachment metadata. If significant drop, metadata is important.

critic_r1_output.summary: The PoC contains two FATAL flaws in temporal/campaign split logic and label completeness that could invalidate test set validity. Several MATERIAL issues regarding baseline fairness, validation stability, and feature configuration further weaken confidence in the conclusions.

defender_r1_output.pass_1_analysis: F1 questions whether temporal splitting and campaign-level stratification can both hold if campaigns span month boundaries; the methodology asserts campaign-level stratification prevents any campaign split across folds, which directly addresses the leakage concern. F2 raises the possibility of incomplete labels due to the 7-day dwell time requirement; the methodology states that only emails with a minimum 7-day dwell time are retained, which ensures label completeness if applied uniformly. F3 argues that the transformer's improvement may stem from text features rather than architecture; the methodology includes an ablation baseline (transformer without body text) to isolate the text contribution, alongside the logistic regression baseline for overall performance. F4 worries that three months of validation data are insufficient for stable hyperparameter selection; the methodology notes the use of cross-validation or repeated holdout on the validation set to stabilize selection. F5 concerns that truncating email body text to 512 tokens might discard important signals; the methodology does not provide analysis of truncation adequacy, leaving the effect uncertain. F6 challenges the verifiability of campaign-level stratification due to undisclosed campaign identification; the methodology asserts the stratification but does not detail how campaigns are defined, leaving the claim unverified. F7 doubts that frozen threat-intelligence scores are sufficient for production deployment; the methodology freezes scores solely to prevent leakage, not to claim production sufficiency, placing the concern outside the experiment's scope. F8 wonders whether limited attachment metadata is sufficient; the methodology does not assert metadata sufficiency, using it merely as a feature set, so the critique targets an out-of-scope assumption.

rebuttals.justification: The design includes campaign-level stratification that explicitly ensures no campaign is split across training, validation, or test sets. This control eliminates campaign leakage by construction, regardless of whether campaigns cross month boundaries, directly addressing the failure mechanism.

The methodology retains only emails with a minimum 7-day dwell time, meaning label timestamps are at least seven days after the email timestamp. This filter guarantees label completeness for all retained emails, including the test set, countering the incomplete-label concern.

An ablation baseline (transformer without body text) isolates the contribution of text modeling, while the logistic regression baseline evaluates overall performance. Together these controls distinguish the effect of text + metadata from architecture alone, addressing the ambiguity concern.
Hyperparameter tuning uses cross-validation or repeated holdout on the validation set (months 13–15) to stabilize selection, mitigating overfitting risk despite the limited validation duration.

To determine whether 512-token truncation discards predictive signals, we should examine misclassified emails for length. If failures correlate with lengths >512 tokens, truncation is problematic; otherwise, it is sufficient. This empirical test is needed because the methodology does not provide truncation adequacy analysis.

The claim that campaign-level stratification prevents any campaign split hinges on how campaigns are identified. We request the campaign identification methodology (e.g., based on thread IDs, subject hashes, or threat-intelligence tags) and verification that no campaign spans the temporal boundaries. Without this, the claim remains unverified.

The experiment freezes threat-intelligence scores solely to prevent future data leakage during training; it does not claim that frozen scores are sufficient for production deployment with dynamic intelligence. Thus the critique concerns production generalization, which is outside the stated scope of validating transformer-based text modeling versus the baseline.

The methodology uses attachment metadata as a feature set but does not assert that this metadata alone is sufficient for phishing detection. The critique's question about metadata sufficiency targets an out-of-scope assumption; the experiment's conclusions are about the added value of text modeling, not the adequacy of metadata.

defender_r1_output.verdict_rationale: Two findings (F5 and F6) remain unresolved and require empirical tests (DEFER), while all other findings are rebutted via design or scope arguments. No conceded finding exceeds adjusted severity 3, satisfying the conditions for empirical_test_agreed.

challenges.reasoning: The rebuttal asserts campaign stratification prevents splits but does not explain how this is reconciled with the temporal split when campaigns span month boundaries. If campaigns cross months 12-13 or 15-16, the temporal split must be violated to keep campaigns together, or the stratification claim is false. The defender's justification ('regardless of whether campaigns cross month boundaries') ignores this logical contradiction without explaining the mechanism.

The filtering mechanism directly addresses the incomplete label concern by ensuring only emails with complete labels (7-day dwell time) are included in the test set. This resolves the leakage risk from incomplete labels.

The ablation baseline (transformer without body text) uses subject line and attachment metadata, not the sender reputation and header features used by the logistic regression baseline. This does not isolate the effect of architecture vs. text features as claimed. The ambiguity between feature sets and architecture remains unresolved.

The rebuttal is generic ('uses cross-validation... to stabilize') and does not provide evidence that 3 months of data is sufficient for stable hyperparameter selection. Cross-validation on a small validation set can still lead to high variance in hyperparameter selection, and no empirical stability analysis is provided.
DEFER rebuttal. The defender correctly identifies that empirical analysis of misclassified emails is needed to determine truncation adequacy. The issue remains open pending this analysis.
DEFER rebuttal. The defender correctly identifies that campaign identification methodology is required to verify the stratification claim. The issue remains open pending this documentation.

The defender correctly identifies that the claim about production sufficiency is out of scope. The experiment only validates model performance vs. baseline, not production deployment conditions with dynamic threat intelligence.
The defender correctly identifies that the claim about metadata sufficiency is out of scope. The experiment focuses on the added value of text modeling, not the adequacy of metadata alone.

defender_r2_output.pass_2_analysis: We accept the critic's challenges on F2, F7, and F8 as correct. For F1 we find the challenge valid and concede the flaw due to lack of a design control for campaign leakage when campaigns cross month boundaries. For F3 and F4 we find the challenges valid and switch to DEFER, pending empirical tests to resolve ambiguity and hyperparameter stability. For F5 and F6 we maintain the DEFER position as the critic's PARTIAL indicates the issue remains open.

rebuttals.justification: The methodology does not provide any explicit mechanism to reconcile the temporal split (months 1-12 train, 13-15 val, 16-18 test) with campaign-level stratification when campaigns span month boundaries (e.g., months 12-13 or 15-16). Without a rule for assigning whole campaigns to a single split, the design cannot guarantee that no campaign is split across folds, leaving the leakage mechanism unaddressed. Therefore we concede the flaw.

The 7-day dwell time filter guarantees that label timestamps are at least seven days after the email timestamp, ensuring label completeness for all retained emails, including the test set. This directly eliminates the incomplete-label concern, as accepted by the critic.

The ablation baseline (transformer without body text) uses subject line and attachment metadata, while the logistic regression baseline uses sender reputation and header features, leaving the ambiguity between architecture and text unresolved. To settle, we will train a transformer with the same features as the baseline (sender reputation, header flags) and compare its PR-AUC to the baseline; if performance differs, architecture contributes, else text drives the improvement. The primary conclusion (transformer outperforms baseline) remains valid regardless of outcome.

While cross-validation or repeated holdout on the three-month validation set aims to stabilize hyperparameter selection, the limited duration may still yield unstable selections, especially for high-capacity models. We defer to an empirical stability test: compare hyperparameters selected across multiple random splits of the validation set; if selections vary widely, the validation set is insufficient. The primary conclusion could still hold if instability affects both models similarly, but empirical verification is needed.
As previously noted, determining whether 512-token truncation discards predictive signals requires examining misclassified emails for length; we defer to that analysis.

As previously noted, verifying campaign-level stratification requires the campaign identification methodology; we defer to obtaining that documentation and checking that no campaign spans the temporal boundaries.

The experiment's scope is to compare transformer-based text modeling against the sender-reputation/header baseline; it does not claim that frozen threat-intelligence scores are sufficient for production deployment with dynamic intelligence, so the critique is out of scope.
The experiment does not assert that attachment metadata alone is sufficient for phishing detection; it evaluates the added value of text modeling, so the critique is out of scope.

defender_r2_output.verdict_rationale: We conceded a fatal flaw (F1) that remains severe and would materially affect the primary metric, while other uncertainties remain open for empirical test, leading to a critique-winning outcome.
